frontmatter.title: Model Comparison: Classifying Learning Strategies in the TAMBI Task

children.value: Participants in the TAMBI mosquito defense task face a complex learning problem: they must infer hidden environmental regularities - latent state transitions, cue-dependent directional offsets, and the distinction between noise and genuine change - from noisy, intermittent outcome feedback. Not all participants approach this problem in the same way. Some may engage in sophisticated latent structure inference, tracking hidden states and attributing surprises to their appropriate sources. Others may rely on simpler strategies: following the most recent outcome direction, or learning the association between cue colours and attack directions without ever building a model of the latent state dynamics. A small number may not engage meaningfully at all.
Before we can interpret the cognitive parameters estimated by our inference agent (
which type of learning strategy each participant is using
. This is essential for three reasons:

The four cognitive parameters (precision weighting, noise reactivity, belief momentum, perceived volatility) are defined within a latent-structure-learning agent. Estimating these parameters for a participant who is not performing structure learning would be scientifically inappropriate (analogous to fitting a linear regression model to non-linear data)

Participants who clicked randomly or without meaningful engagement should be identified and excluded from subsequent analyses. Here, model comparison provides an objective, data-driven exclusion criterion.
Characterisation of the learning strategy spectrum.

The distribution of winning models across participants provides insight into the prevalence of different learning strategies in the sample, and can be related to instruction condition and trait-level measures.

(Radev et al., 2023; Schmitt et al., 2023) to perform amortized Bayesian model comparison. The approach follows the same simulation-based inference logic as the parameter estimation pipeline: a neural classifier is trained on a large corpus of simulated behavioral data (generated from all candidate models), and then applied to real participant data to estimate posterior model probabilities.
The key advantage over traditional model comparison methods (e.g., BIC, AIC, Bayes factors via bridge sampling) is that amortized model comparison:
Operates on the same 40 behavioral summary features used for parameter estimation (ensuring consistency)
Provides well-calibrated posterior probabilities over models (not just point estimates or ranking)
Requires no per-participant optimization or sampling at inference time (~1 ms per participant)
Can be validated via confusion matrices, calibration curves, and model recovery analyses before application to real data

Rather than comparing a large number of models that differ in subtle ways, we define a compact set of four models that span the qualitative spectrum from disengaged behavior to full latent structure inference. This design reflects a principled ablation logic: each successive model adds a qualitatively distinct cognitive capability.

The rationale for limiting the set to four models is twofold. First, the three cognitive transitions (no learning → outcome tracking → cue association → structure inference) capture the most important qualitative distinctions in learning strategy. Second, model variants that differ only in continuous parameter settings within the structure-learning agent (e.g. with or without oddball attribution, with fixed or free volatility) are better handled by the parameter estimation pipeline, where these differences manifest as different parameter values rather than as discrete model classes. Maintaining them as separate models would constitute “double dipping” - testing the same cognitive dimension at both the model and parameter level.
Participant exclusion criterion. Identifies data that are indistinguishable from random allocation.

On each trial, 7 nets are placed by drawing 7 slot indices uniformly at random (with replacement) from the 32 available slots. This produces allocations where each slot has approximately equal probability of receiving a net, with stochastic variation in the number of nets per slot due to the multinomial sampling.
High entropy, low defense rate, zero autocorrelation, no systematic response to oddballs or transitions.
Tests whether behavior can be explained by simple exponential moving average (EMA) tracking of the most recent outcome direction, without any cue differentiation or latent state inference.

What this model adds over M_TRACK_SIMPLE:
The ability to place nets at different angular positions depending on the cue colour - a directly observable regularity in the task.
Latent state transitions (the agent has no concept of alternating hidden states), oddball attribution (all surprises are treated as signal), or episode-boundary adaptation.

The primary model. Tests whether behaviour reflects latent structure inference - tracking hidden states, attributing surprises to oddball noise versus genuine change, and adapting beliefs after state transitions.

The full reduced-form inference agent as described in the Parameter Estimation chapter. Implements a two-state Hidden Markov Model with cue-dependent offsets, oddball mixture observation model, and belief momentum.
precision_weighting, noise_reactivity, belief_momentum, h_switch (4 parameters total). See the Parameter Estimation chapter for complete specification.

children.value: , producing systematic direction changes that the agent can anticipate. The oddball mixture that allows the agent to separate noise from signal. The belief momentum that captures individual differences in adaptation speed.
The four models form a nested hierarchy of cognitive capabilities:

A participant classified as M_STRUCT is not only performing latent structure inference, but is doing so in a way that is better captured by the structure-learning model than by simpler alternatives - even after accounting for the additional free parameters (since BayesFlow model comparison implicitly penalises model complexity via the prior predictive distribution).
All four models produce integer net allocations

children.value: on every trial. These allocations are processed through the same 40-feature extraction pipeline used for parameter estimation (see Appendix A of the
The classifier sees the same feature representation for training (simulated data from all four models) and inference (real participant data).
A participant classified as M_STRUCT by the model comparison will have their subsequent parameter estimates computed from the same features.
The features that drive classification can be inspected directly (e.g. “M_RANDOM is distinguished from M_TRACK_SIMPLE primarily by low autocorrelation and high entropy”).
, which implements amortized Bayesian model comparison via a neural classifier trained on simulated data from all candidate models.

children.html: R1→R4\mathbb{R}^1 \to \mathbb{R}^4R1→R4

children.value: processes the feature vector (reshaped to a 1-element set). The DeepSet maps the 40-dimensional feature vector to an 8-dimensional embedding.
An MLP with two hidden layers of 64 units each (SiLU activation, no dropout) maps the 8-dimensional embedding to 4 logits, which are passed through a softmax to produce posterior model probabilities.

The total number of trainable parameters is approximately 8,000 (which is quite small - this is intentional and allows to prevent overfitting, given the relatively low complexity of the 4-class classification problem).

children.html: N=40,000N = 40{,}000N=40,000

children.value: simulations are generated in parallel (10,000 per model, balanced). For each simulation:
A model is selected uniformly at random from {M_RANDOM, M_TRACK_SIMPLE, M_TRACK_CUE, M_STRUCT}
Parameters are drawn from the model’s prior
A trial schedule is randomly selected from the 20 available schedules
The model simulates the full session
Class balance is maintained by uniform model sampling during generation.
The classifier is trained via cross-entropy loss for 200 epochs with:

children.value: Model comparison validation is more extensive than parameter recovery validation, because misclassification has direct consequences for participant inclusion/exclusion and for the interpretation of subsequent parameter estimates. We perform five complementary validation analyses.

A balanced test set (1,000 simulations per model = 4,000 total) is generated from fresh parameter draws and schedules not used in training. For each simulation, the classifier predicts the model with highest posterior probability. The confusion matrix shows, for each true model, the fraction of simulations classified as each predicted model.

children.html: >0.80> 0.80>0.80

children.value: for all models. Off-diagonal entries should be small and concentrated on “adjacent” models in the ablation hierarchy (e.g. some M_TRACK_CUE simulations misclassified as M_TRACK_SIMPLE, but very few M_RANDOM simulations misclassified as M_STRUCT).

children.alt: The confusion matrix shows r = 1.0, which corresponds to 100% accuracy.

children.value: The confusion matrix shows r = 1.0, which corresponds to 100% accuracy.
5.2 Calibration Curves and Expected Calibration Error

children.label: 5.2 Calibration Curves and Expected Calibration Error

children.value: For each model, we compute a reliability diagram, showing the relationship between the classifier’s predicted probability (confidence) and the empirical frequency of correct classification. A well-calibrated classifier has predicted probability equal to empirical accuracy across all confidence levels.
(ECE) quantifies the deviation from perfect calibration:

For each pair of models, we show the discriminability (how well can the classifier distinguish them when only those two models are considered?) by computing the binary classification accuracy (does the classifier assign higher probability to the correct model?) and visualise the distributions of

children.html: p(modeli)p(\text{model}_i)p(modeli​)

children.value: This analysis identifies whether some model pairs are (partially) confused.

For M_STRUCT specifically we check whether the classifier’s accuracy depend on the parameter values used to generate the data. This is central to evaluate whether some parameter regions produce behaviour that is indistinguishable from simpler models (and thus inducing identifyability issues). For example, M_STRUCT with very low noise reactivity and high belief momentum might resemble M_TRACK_CUE, because the agent rarely switches states. Conversely, M_STRUCT with very high h_switch might produce behaviour so variable that it resembles M_RANDOM.

We partition the parameter space into quintiles for each of the four M_STRUCT parameters and compute classification accuracy within each quintile. This identifies potential “blind spots” in the classification and informs the interpretation of boundary cases in real participant data.
However, this is not the case for our models as this figure shows.

children.alt: Literally perfect accuracy across parameter values.

children.value: Literally perfect accuracy across parameter values.
Net allocations are parsed from the behavioural results CSV
The 40 behavioural features are extracted (identical pipeline to training data)
The feature vector is passed through the trained classifier
Posterior probabilities over the four models are returned
A participant is flagged for exclusion if

mmm

children.value: (i.e. the random model has the highest posterior probability). That means, a participant is only excluded when their behaviour is genuinely better described by random allocation than by any form of directed learning.
The model comparison and parameter estimation pipelines are designed to be used sequentially:
Participants classified as M_STRUCT have their

Participants classified as M_TRACK_SIMPLE or M_TRACK_CUE are reported as using simpler strategies; their parameter estimates (if computed) should be interpreted with caution, as the structure-learning parameters (noise_reactivity, belief_momentum, h_switch) are not meaningful for agents that do not perform structure learning
This sequential design ensures that cognitive parameters are only interpreted for participants whose behaviour is consistent with the generating model of those parameters.
7. Relation to the Instruction Condition Manipulation

children.label: 7. Relation to the Instruction Condition Manipulation

children.value: The three instruction conditions create different expectations about the model comparison results:

Participants must discover all regularities through experience. We expect the full spectrum, including some M_TRACK_SIMPLE (slow learners), some M_TRACK_CUE (learn cue association but not state transitions), and some M_STRUCT (full structure learning).

Participants are told that cue colours are associated with different directions but not given specific values. We expect fewer M_TRACK_SIMPLE (the cue association is primed) and more M_TRACK_CUE or M_STRUCT.
Participants are given the exact cue offset (70°) and state rotation (120°). We expect predominantly M_STRUCT, because the explicit rule knowledge should enable rapid structure learning.
The model comparison thus provides a manipulation check (does the instruction condition shift participants toward more sophisticated learning strategies, as intended?)
Radev, S. T., Schmitt, M., Schumacher, L., Elsemüller, L., Pratz, V., Schälte, Y., ... & Bürkner, P. C. (2023). BayesFlow: Amortized Bayesian workflows with neural networks.
Schmitt, M., Bürkner, P. C., Köthe, U., & Radev, S. T. (2023). Detecting model misspecification in amortized Bayesian inference with neural networks.
Talts, S., Betancourt, M., Simpson, D., Vehtari, A., & Gelman, A. (2018). Validating Bayesian inference algorithms with simulation-based calibration.
Zaheer, M., Kottur, S., Ravanbakhsh, S., Poczos, B., Salakhutdinov, R. R., & Smola, A. J. (2017). Deep sets.
